questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a client with a chest tube notes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.text: Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak.
Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes.
Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber.
Strip the tubing toward the drainage system.

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak somewhere in the system. The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Prolonged clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax, and stripping the tubing is not recommended because it can increase intrathoracic pressure.

distractorRationales.b: Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak priority points to Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak priority points to Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Strip the tubing toward the drainage system misses the clinical pattern: Chest Tube Air Leak priority points to Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Continuous water-seal bubbling means assess the system for an air leak before doing anything that could trap air in the chest.

questions.stem: A toddler with croup has a barky cough and now has inspiratory stridor at rest. Which finding most concerns the nurse?

options.text: Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing.
Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests.
A barking cough that occurs mainly at night.
Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C).

questions.rationale: Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness alone. The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress to fatigue and failure. Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.

distractorRationales.b: Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests misses the clinical pattern: Croup Stridor Priority airway points to Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A barking cough that occurs mainly at night misses the clinical pattern: Croup Stridor Priority airway points to Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C) misses the clinical pattern: Croup Stridor Priority airway points to Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction.

questions.stem: An older adult who was oriented this morning is now disorganized, trying to climb out of bed, and seeing insects on the wall. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment.
This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning.
The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia.
This behavior is best managed by limiting all stimulation and delaying assessment.

questions.rationale: A rapid change in mental status with agitation and visual hallucinations is more consistent with delirium than chronic dementia. Delirium can be triggered by infection, hypoxia, medications, or metabolic problems and requires prompt assessment for reversible causes. Delaying evaluation increases the risk that a treatable medical problem will be missed.

distractorRationales.b: This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning misses the clinical pattern: Delirium Vs Dementia Acute hypoxia points to The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia misses the clinical pattern: Delirium Vs Dementia Acute hypoxia points to The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: This behavior is best managed by limiting all stimulation and delaying assessment misses the clinical pattern: Delirium Vs Dementia Acute hypoxia points to The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Sudden confusion and hallucinations in an older adult point to delirium until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A client taking digoxin reports nausea, poor appetite, and seeing yellow halos around lights. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the digoxin and assess the apical pulse.
Give the dose with food to reduce gastric upset.
Teach the client that visual changes are expected.
Administer an antacid and reassess in 1 hour.

questions.rationale: Nausea and yellow visual halos are classic early signs of digoxin toxicity. The nurse should withhold the medication and assess the apical pulse before giving any additional doses. Telling the client the findings are expected or simply treating stomach upset misses a potentially dangerous cardiac medication problem.

distractorRationales.b: Give the dose with food to reduce gastric upset misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity cardiac points to Hold the digoxin and assess the apical pulse, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Teach the client that visual changes are expected misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity cardiac points to Hold the digoxin and assess the apical pulse, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Administer an antacid and reassess in 1 hour misses the clinical pattern: Digoxin Toxicity cardiac points to Hold the digoxin and assess the apical pulse, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Suspected digoxin toxicity means hold the dose first and assess before giving more.

questions.stem: A child arrives with fever, drooling, muffled voice, and inspiratory stridor. Which action by the nurse is the priority?

options.text: Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management.
Inspect the throat with a tongue blade for redness.
Obtain a throat culture before notifying the provider.
Lay the child flat for a full respiratory assessment.

questions.rationale: Drooling, stridor, and a muffled voice suggest epiglottitis, which can lead to sudden airway obstruction. The nurse should keep the child calm, avoid upsetting airway manipulation, and prepare for expert airway management. Using a tongue blade or forcing the child flat can precipitate complete obstruction.

distractorRationales.b: Inspect the throat with a tongue blade for redness misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Obtain a throat culture before notifying the provider misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Lay the child flat for a full respiratory assessment misses the clinical pattern: Epiglottitis Airway Protection airway points to Keep the child calm and prepare for emergency airway management, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Suspected epiglottitis is an airway emergency: do not inspect the throat and do prepare for airway management.

questions.stem: A laboring client receiving oxytocin has recurrent late fetal heart rate decelerations. Which nursing action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the oxytocin infusion.
Prepare for an immediate vaginal examination.
Increase the oxytocin to improve labor progress.
Encourage the client to begin pushing with each contraction.

questions.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations suggest uteroplacental insufficiency, and oxytocin can worsen that pattern by increasing uterine activity. The first nursing move is to stop the oxytocin, then reposition the client, increase IV fluids, and notify the provider as needed. Increasing stimulation or encouraging pushing can further reduce fetal oxygen delivery.

distractorRationales.b: Prepare for an immediate vaginal examination misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Increase the oxytocin to improve labor progress misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Encourage the client to begin pushing with each contraction misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue oxygen points to Stop the oxytocin infusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Late decelerations with oxytocin on board mean remove the stressor first by stopping the infusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A postpartum client receiving magnesium sulfate for preeclampsia has respirations of 10/min, absent patellar reflexes, and urine output of 20 mL/hr. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider.
Administer the next scheduled antihypertensive dose.
Place the client flat and encourage rest.
Reassess reflexes after the client has been sleeping for 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression, absent reflexes, and oliguria are classic toxicity findings with magnesium sulfate. The drug should be stopped immediately and the provider notified so antidote and further management can be started. Waiting or focusing on less urgent medications allows magnesium levels to continue rising and increases the risk of arrest.

distractorRationales.b: Administer the next scheduled antihypertensive dose misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client flat and encourage rest misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Reassess reflexes after the client has been sleeping for 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity respiratory points to Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Low respirations, absent reflexes, and low urine output on magnesium sulfate point to toxicity and require the infusion to be stopped first.

questions.stem: A postoperative client received IV morphine 20 minutes ago. The nurse finds the client difficult to arouse with respirations of 8/min. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone.
Document that the client is sleeping after pain relief.
Place a warm blanket on the client and reassess in 30 minutes.
Ask the client to rate pain before intervening.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression after opioid administration is a priority safety emergency. The nurse should stop additional opioid exposure, stimulate the client, support breathing, and prepare naloxone per protocol. Comfort-focused reassessment without addressing respirations delays treatment of an unstable airway and ventilation problem.

distractorRationales.b: Document that the client is sleeping after pain relief misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Place a warm blanket on the client and reassess in 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Ask the client to rate pain before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation airway points to Stop further opioid dosing and stimulate the client while preparing naloxone, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Difficulty arousing plus slow respirations after opioids means act for oversedation before anything else.
